metadata.seed: I keep overthinking everything at work, and I'm scared I'm going to get fired even though nobody said anything.

patient.query: I keep overthinking everything at work, and I'm scared I'm going to get fired even though nobody said anything.

automatic_thoughts: I'm scared I'm going to get fired

entailment: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re worried about being fired and that you’re overthinking a lot. What evidence do you have that this will happen? And what evidence suggests it might not?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing
retrieved_concepts_used.label: entailment

patient.query: Yeah, I guess I’m just really anxious about my performance lately. I can’t shake off this feeling that I’m not doing enough, even though my boss hasn’t said anything directly. I keep replaying my last meeting in my mind, thinking I messed up something important. But, I mean, I’ve received good feedback before… it just feels like it could all change in an instant.

automatic_thoughts: it could all change in an instant

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re worried that a single slip could change everything, even though you’ve received positive feedback before. What evidence do you have that you’ve actually fallen short, and what evidence suggests you’ve met or exceeded expectations?

patient.query: I know, deep down, that I’ve received compliments and praise for my work. I can see those good moments, but when I make a mistake, it just feels so much heavier, you know? Like, my mind just fixates on that one misstep, and it overshadows everything else. It’s almost like I’m waiting for someone to call me out, even when things seem

automatic_thoughts: my mind just fixates on that one misstep
I’m waiting for someone to call me out

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: What evidence do you have that this one misstep is more important than the praise you’ve received? Can you think of a time when you felt proud of your work even after a mistake? How might you view the mistake differently if you considered it as a learning moment rather than a defining flaw?

patient.query: It's hard to think about it that way. I mean, I know I’ve had moments where I felt proud, and I guess some mistakes didn’t derail everything. But when I mess up, it just sticks with me. I keep replaying it in my head, and it feels like it defines me in that moment. I want to see it as a learning moment, but it’s tough when

automatic_thoughts: it feels like it defines me in that moment
behaviors: keep replaying it in my head

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re holding a strong belief that a single mistake defines you. What evidence do you have that supports that idea? And what evidence might suggest that it doesn’t? Exploring both sides could help you see the situation from a broader perspective.

patient.query: I guess... I’ve always been really hard on myself. I mean, my parents were pretty strict, and it felt like I had to be perfect to get their approval. So, I think that sticks with me even now. But looking back, I’ve had plenty of times where I made a mistake and everything turned out okay. People move on, and I’ve moved on too. Still,

neutral: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)
Tends to be sensitive to criticism (finding)
Tends to fail to learn from adverse experience (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’ve noticed that mistakes haven’t always led to negative outcomes. What evidence do you have that being perfect is necessary for approval? And what evidence suggests that people still value you even when you’re not perfect?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Tends to be sensitive to criticism
retrieved_concepts_used.label: neutral

patient.query: Well, I guess when I think about it, my parents praised me more when I did well, and they didn’t respond the same way when I messed up. So it’s hard to shake that feeling that I need to be perfect to be liked or to be valued. But, there have definitely been times when friends and coworkers seemed to appreciate me for who I am, not just my accomplishments.

automatic_thoughts: I need to be perfect to be liked

neutral: Tends to appear indifferent to praise or criticism (finding)
Character trait finding of level of pride (finding)
Tends to be critical of others (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’ve noticed a pattern where praise feels tied to flawless performance, yet you’ve also seen people value you for who you are. What evidence do you have that being imperfect actually hurts how others see you, and what evidence suggests they appreciate you regardless of mistakes? You might try framing your worth as something that can coexist with both effort and authenticity.

patient.query: I mean, I get that in theory, but it’s just hard to really believe it. I keep thinking that if people see me fail—even just once—they’ll start to doubt my abilities or my character. But then, I think about my friends who have messed up and how I didn’t think less of them. I guess I just wish I could internalize that for myself. It feels...

automatic_thoughts: if people see me fail, they’ll start to doubt my abilities or my character
I wish I could internalize that for myself

neutral: Character trait finding of ability to learn from adverse experience (finding)

llm_response.response: What evidence do you have that people will doubt you if you fail? Can you think of times when someone else failed and people still respected or supported them? How might you test whether that belief holds true in your own situation?

patient.query: I don’t know, it just feels so real to me. I hear that voice in my head telling me I’ll be a disappointment. I know I’ve seen friends bounce back from their mistakes and still be supported, but for some reason, I can’t quite connect that to myself. It just feels scarier when I think about my own failures. I guess testing it? It seems daunting...

automatic_thoughts: I can’t quite connect that to myself

neutral: Tends to fail to learn from adverse experience (finding)
Loss of hope for the future (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like that inner voice is really powerful right now. What specific moments or thoughts make you feel you might disappoint yourself? And how might you view those moments differently to see a more hopeful possibility?

patient.query: Yeah, that voice can be relentless. I think about times when I’ve had to present something and I stumbled over my words, or when a project didn’t go as planned. It feels like everyone is judging me in those moments. But I guess, if I were to think differently, maybe I could see those slip-ups as just part of a larger journey. It’s just… hard to shift
